mnemonics.title: Master Theorem Cases
mnemonics.content: P-L-E: Power (Case 1), Log (Case 2), Exponential (Case 3) - Remember: Compare f(n) with n^(log_b a)

mnemonics.title: Recurrence Tree Levels
mnemonics.content: At each level i: problem size = n/b^i, number of subproblems = a^i, work per node = f(n/b^i)

acronyms.expansion: Time complexity function of input size n
acronyms.usage: Used to express running time of algorithm recursively
Both upper and lower bound: T(n)=Θ(f(n)) means c1f(n) ≤ T(n) ≤ c2f(n)
T(n)=O(f(n)) means T(n) grows no faster than f(n)
T(n)=Ω(f(n)) means T(n) grows at least as fast as f(n)

visualTips.visualization: Draw triangle with root at top. Each level splits into 'a' branches, problem size shrinks by 'b'. Sum all level costs.
Three buckets: Case 1 (subproblems win), Case 2 (balanced), Case 3 (combine work wins). Compare exponents of n.
Unfold recurrence like accordion: T(n) → T(n/b) → T(n/b²) → ... until base case, then sum all work.

analogies.analogy: Like a family tree: root is original problem, children are subproblems, each generation has more people but smaller tasks.
Like comparing three runners: subproblems (n^log_b a) vs combine work f(n). Who finishes first determines complexity.
Like finding word in dictionary by opening to middle each time - cuts search space in half each step.
Like organizing playing cards: split into individual cards, then merge back in sorted order.
